coredata.dc:title: “On-off-on” fluorescent system for detection of Zn2+ in biological samples using quantum dots-carbon dots ratiometric nanosensor
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Song, Quan
Ma, Yunsu
Wang, Xu
Tang, Tang
Song, Yueyue
Ma, Yujie
Xu, Guanhong
Wei, Fangdi
Cen, Yao
Hu, Qin
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a novel strategy for Zn2+ detection was established by using an “on-off-on” ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor with the help of ethylene diaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), a common metal chelating agent. The simple and practical ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor was synthesized by covalently binding CdTe quantum dots (QDs) and carbon dots (CDs), possessing two emission peaks at 525 nm and 450 nm under a single wavelength excitation of 360 nm, respectively. The CdTe QDs served as the response signal label, and the CDs, having no response to the analytes, acted as the reference signal. The fluorescence emission of the nanosensor was turned off to create a low-level “off” state when EDTA was added, and turned on dramatically with the addition of the target Zn2+. Under the optimal conditions, the change of the fluorescence intensity ratio of the nanosensor at 525 nm and 450 nm (ΔF525/F450) had good linearity against the concentrations of Zn2+ within a dynamic linear range of 0.50–40 μM. The limit of detection was as low as 0.33 μM (3σ/K), which was low enough for the detection of Zn2+ in human body. The proposed method was successfully applied to the detection of Zn2+ in human urine and plasma with RSDs less than 10%. The results show that the as-prepared QDs-CDs ratiometric nanosensor has potential application of clinical detection of Zn2+ in the human body.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dots
Carbon dots
Ratiometric fluorescence
Zinc detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of QDs-CDs ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor
Detection of Zn2+ using QDs-CDs ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor
Application of the as-prepared ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor
Fabrication of QDs-CDs ratiometric nanosensor and its working principle for Zn2+ detection
Stability of QDs-CDs ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor
The influence of EDTA on the detection of Zn2+
Selectivity of the QDs-CDs ratiometric nanosensor
Detection of Zn2+ using the QDs-CDs ratiometric nanosensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence emission spectra (λex = 360 nm) of CdTe QDs (a), CDs (b) and QDs-CDs ratiometric nanosensor (c). The inset shows the corresponding fluorescence photos under a 365 nm UV lamp.
TEM image of QDs-CDs ratiometric nanosensor, the inset image is high-resolution TEM.
(A) FT-IR spectra of QDs, CDs, QDs-CDs nanosensor and mixture of QDs and CDs. (B) Absorption spectra of QDs, CDs, QDs-CDs nanosensor and mixture of QDs and CDs.
(A) Fluorescence properties in different pH. (B) Fluorescence properties in different pH for different time.

Fluorescence recovery of the QDs-CDs quenched by different concentrations of EDTA after the addition of 20 μM Zn2+, the inset image showed fluorescence intensity of the QDs-CDs after the addition of different concentrations of EDTA.
The selectivity of the ratiometric nanosensor to various metal ions and other interferent.

(A) The corresponding fluorescence spectra of the ratiometric nanosensor with the addition of different concentrations of Zn2+. The inset image showed plot of the fluorescence intensity ratio change as a function of the concentration of Zn2+. (B) The corresponding fluorescence photos with different concentrations of Zn2+.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of the QDs-CDs ratiometric nanosensor and the sensing mechanism for Zn2+ detection.
Precision of Zn2+ determination by QDs-CDs ratiometric nanosensor (n = 3).
Determination of Zn2+ in urine and serum samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: “On-off-on” fluorescent system for detection of Zn2+ in biological samples using quantum dots-carbon dots ratiometric nanosensor

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing, Jiangsu 211166, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel strategy for ratiometric detection of Zn2+ was established based on the “on-off-on” ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor using the coupled quantum dots-carbon dots.

In this work, a novel strategy for Zn2+ detection was established by using an “on-off-on” ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor with the help of ethylene diaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), a common metal chelating agent. The simple and practical ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor was synthesized by covalently binding CdTe quantum dots (QDs) and carbon dots (CDs), possessing two emission peaks at 525 nm and 450 nm under a single wavelength excitation of 360 nm, respectively. The CdTe QDs served as the response signal label, and the CDs, having no response to the analytes, acted as the reference signal. The fluorescence emission of the nanosensor was turned off to create a low-level “off” state when EDTA was added, and turned on dramatically with the addition of the target Zn2+. Under the optimal conditions, the change of the fluorescence intensity ratio of the nanosensor at 525 nm and 450 nm (ΔF525/F450) had good linearity against the concentrations of Zn2+ within a dynamic linear range of 0.50–40 μM. The limit of detection was as low as 0.33 μM (3σ/K), which was low enough for the detection of Zn2+ in human body. The proposed method was successfully applied to the detection of Zn2+ in human urine and plasma with RSDs less than 10%. The results show that the as-prepared QDs-CDs ratiometric nanosensor has potential application of clinical detection of Zn2+ in the human body.

ce:section.ce:para: Zinc, one of the essential trace elements in human body, is second only to ferrum in content [1,2]. As key part of the structural components of a large number of metalloenzyme, zinc plays a very important part in terms of biological functions, including cell proliferation and differentiation, gene expression, signal transduction and neurotransmission [3,4]. Besides, some studies indicate that disruption of zinc homeostasis is responsible to several diseases, such as Alzheimer’s [4,5], epilepsy [6], diabetes [7] and cancer. So, detection of zinc in human body is an important way to diagnosis these diseases in clinic. Traditional methods for Zn2+ detection include atomic absorption spectrometry [8,9], dithizone colorimetric method [10,11], anodic stripping voltammetry [12,13], and oscillographic polarography [14]. Certainly, there are some other ways to detect zinc, such as optodes [15], electrochemical sensor [16] and flourometric sensor [17]. However, these methods either are sensitive and selective but laboratory-based andtedious in sample preparation, or are easy to operate but weak in sensitivity and selectivity. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop novel approaches that are convenient and fast as well as sensitive and selective.

Recently, fluorescent nanosensors, like quantum dots (QDs) and carbon dots (CDs) have drawn more and more attentions due to their unique optical characteristics [18-21]. Compared with traditional organic fluorescent dyes, QDs and CDs possess remarkable advantages, such as size-tunable optical properties, high fluorescent quantum yields and excellent photochemical stability [18,22]. As individual fluorescent nanosensors, both QDs and CDs have good performance in detection of different analytes. In recent years, some researchers have established ratiometric fluorescent methods based on QDs with CDs to obtain more sensitive and selective fluorescent nanosensors, attributed to the decrease of error caused by the interference of instrumental efficiency and environmental conditions, such as the instability of the light source, the changes of temperature and pH when measuring. For example, Benmei Cao and co-workers [23] developed a ratiometric fluorescence probe for the detection of Hg2+, Yehan Yan and co-workers [24] synthesized a ratiometric fluorescence probe for the detection of NO2 in aqueous solution, Yahui Wang and co-workers [25] utilized hybrid QDs-CDs for the visual detection of copper ions. Despite of the advantages, these ratiometric nanosensors were mostly based on “on-off ” systems,the sensitivity was reduced, resulting from the high-level “on” background signals [26,27]. Thus, an “off-on” system would be a good strategy to improve sensitivity because of the low-level “off” state.

In this paper, we report a new “on-off-on” ratiometric fluorescent method for the detection of zinc in biological samples, based on the connection between CDs and QDs. In general, zinc has no influence on the fluorescence of CDs or QDs. Therefore, EDTA was used to quench the nanosensor to create a very low-level “off” state, building a bridge connecting the fluorescence signals of the QDs-CDs with the concentration of zinc [28]. With the addition of zinc, the fluorescence emission of the nanosensor was turned on reversely. Finally, the turned-on ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor was successfully applied to the detection of zinc in human urine and plasma.

Tellurium powder (purity > 99%), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), cadmium acetate dehydrate ((CH3COO)2Cd⋅2H2O), 3-mercapto propionic acid (MPA), sodium hydroxide, citric acid monohydrate (CA⋅H2O), diethylenetriamine (DETA), zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2⋅6H2O), ethylene diaminetetraaceticacid (EDTA), were purchased from Wanqing chemical industry (Nanjing, China). 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were purchased from Aladdin industrial corporation (Shanghai, China). Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, 5 mM, pH9) contained 0.1 M NaCl. All chemical reagents and solvents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. The purified water used in all experiments was prepared using Direct-Q water purification system (Millipore, USA).

FT-IR spectra were recorded on a TENSOR 27 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) with a resolution of 2 cm−1 and a spectral range of 4000–400 cm−1. Fluorescence spectra were recorded with an F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) with the excitation and emission slit widths at 5 nm. UV–visible absorption spectra were measured with a UV2450 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The transmission electron microscopic images (TEM) were acquired on a JEOL JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (Tokyo, Japan).

Amine-modified CDs were synthesized according to the previously methods [29,30]. Briefly, 1.2 g of citric acid monohydrate and 600 μL of DETA were mixed in 20 mL of ultrapure water. Then the mixture was transferred to a Teflon equipped stainless steel autoclave (30 mL), which was placed in a drying oven, followed by hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C for 4 h. When cooled down to room temperature, the product was concentrated by rotary evaporation, following by purifying with acetone three times. The final product was dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water for further use.

Carboxyl modified CdTe QDs were synthesized following a method in the literature [31]. Briefly, freshly prepared oxygen-free NaHTe solution was added to a Cd2+ aqueous solution at pH 9–10 in the presence of MPA, a stabilizer, under a flow of N2. Then, the mixture was heated to reflux in an oil bath at 90 °C for 6 h. The molar ratio of Cd:Te:MPA was 1:0.5:2.4 in the experiment. To clear the residues, the product was concentrated by rotary evaporation, followed by purification with ethanol. Finally, the obtained CdTe QDs were redispersed in ultrapure water for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of QDs-CDs ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The QDs-CDs ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor was obtained through a coupling reaction between the amine groups of the CDs and the carboxyl groups of the QDs [25,32]. In more detail, 10 mL of 2 mM purified CdTe QDs was mixed with 5 mL of 20 mM EDC/NHS in a flask and stirred for 15 min to activate the carboxyl groups. Next, 100 μL of the amine-modified CDs was added to the above mixture, and then the mixture was stirred for 4 h in the dark. The resulting QDs-CDs nanosensor was centrifuged, washed with ethanol three times and redispersed in PBS (pH = 9).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Zn2+ using QDs-CDs ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, 245 μL of 1 mM QDs-CDs nanosensor was mixed with 245 μL of 1.3 μM EDTA solution in a centrifuge tube. The mixture was shaken slightly for 15 min at room temperature, then, 10 μL of a series of Zn2+ standard solution were added into the mixture, respectively. The final concentrations of Zn2+ were 0.50, 1.0, 2.0, 5.0, 10, 15, 20, 30 and 40 μM. After incubation for 30 min at room temperature, the fluorescence intensities of the mixture were recorded at 525 nm and 450 nm under a single wavelength excitation of 360 nm. Control experiments were carried out under similar conditions in the absence of Zn2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the as-prepared ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The contents of Zn2+ in human urine and plasma were detected by using standard addition method. The urine samples were obtained from healthy laboratory staff volunteers and the plasma samples were from Jiangsu province hospital. The preparation of urine samples was very simple, only needed centrifugation. The plasma samples were prepared as follows. 800 μL of acetonitrile was added to 200 μL of the plasma in an eppendorf tube, vortex-mixed for 30 s and centrifuged at 15,000×g for 15 min at 4 °C. The urine and plasma samples were then spiked with different concentrations of Zn2+ and were characterized as described in Section 2.6.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of QDs-CDs ratiometric nanosensor and its working principle for Zn2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 showed the preparation route of QDs-CDs ratiometric nanosensor and its working principle for Zn2+ detection. The QDs were bound to the CDs by a simple coupling reaction between the carboxyl groups on CdTe QDs and the amino groups on CDs. The obtained ratiometric nanosensor showed dual emission peaks at 525 nm and 450 nm under a single excitation of 360 nm. 3-mercapto propionic acid (MPA) used in the preparation of QDs was the stabilizer of CdTe QDs. There were many Cd–thiol bonds on the surface of MPA modified CdTe QDs. When EDTA was mixed with the nanosensor, it turned off the fluorescence of the QDs-CDs ratiometric nanosensor by breaking the Cd–thiol bonds and forming coordination compound of EDTA-Cd since EDTA easily combines with Cd. When Zn2+ was added into the system, it also easily combined with EDTA because Zn-EDTA and Cd-EDTA have almost the same stability constants of coordination compound, i.e. logK = 16.5 and logK = 16.4, respectively. So, MPA recombined with Cd, and the surface defects of CdTe QDs were repaired. The fluorescence emission of the QDs-CDs ratiometric nanosensor was turned on dramatically. In this whole process, the CDs fluorescence kept unchanged. As a result, the change of the fluorescent intensity ratio (F525/F450) of the nanosensor increased with the increase of Zn2+ concentration.

The fluorescence spectra of the CdTe QDs, CDs and QDs-CDs ratiometric nanosensor are shown in Fig. 1 . It can be seen that the QDs and CDs had a strong fluorescence emission with the maximum wavelength at 525 nm and 450 nm, respectively. The QDs-CDs nanosensor showed maximal emission wavelengths around 525 and 450 nm under a single wavelength excitation at 360 nm, and the nanosensor displayed a bright blue-green color under a UV lamp (Fig. 1c, inset image). The quantum yield of the nanosensor was 73% while rhodamin 6G was used as a standard. Fig. 2 showed TEM of the QDs-CDs ratiometric nanosensor, the average size of the nanosensor was about 3.4 nm, the inset exhibited an identical crystallinity with 0.45 nm lattice spacing under high-resolution TEM. It was observed that the nanosensor was uniform and well dispersed in aqueous solution.

The successful conjugation of the CdTe QDs with the CDs is demonstrated using FT-IR spectra. As shown in Fig. 3 (A), the peak at 1495 cm−1 in the spectra of CDs and mixture of QDs and CDs could be assigned to the N-H deformation vibration. However, it disappeared in the spectrum of QDs-CDs nanosensor, proving the formation of the amide bonds [24,25,30]. The interaction between QDs and CDs was further confirmed by their absorption spectra. It could be seen from Fig. 3(B), the CdTe QDs had an absorption peak at 469 nm [33] while the CDs exhibited two distinct absorption peaks at 239 nm and 351 nm [22,34] respectively. Compared to the absorption of the QDs, there was an obvious red shift from 469 nm to 502 nm in spectrum of the QDs-CDs nanosensor, attributed to the introduction of amide bonds between QDs and CDs [23]. However, the mixture of QDs and CDs did not trigger this change.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of QDs-CDs ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the stability of the ratiometric nanosensor, the effects of pH and storing time on the QDs-CDs nanosensor were studied. It can be seen from Fig. 4 (A) that the fluorescence intensity ratio of the ratiometric nanosensor at two emission peaks (F525/F450) increased with the increase of the pH value from 3 to 6, and kept almost the same in the pH range of 6–8. The ratio was the highest in pH9 solution. The reason may be that in low pH range, the QDs easily aggregated due to the protonation of surface binding thiolates, and their fluorescence intensity was weak [31]. This made the ratios (F525/F450) very low. With the increase of pH, the QDs became stable, and the fluorescence intensity of the QDs was strong, and this made the ratios (F525/F450) high. The ratios (F525/F450) decreased rapidly in the range of pH 9–10, which might be due to the formation of Cd(OH)2 precipitates. The stability of the ratiometric nanosensor in different pH solutions for different storing time was investigated. As shown in Fig. 4(B), the ratios of the nanosensor remained almost unchanged in the pH range from 7.0 to 9.5 for 70 h. In addition, the effect of buffer type on the nanosensor was studied (Fig. S1). The nanosensor had better performance in phosphate buffer. It can be seen that the as-prepared QDs-CDs ratiometric nanosensor had rather good stability and could adapt to broad detection environments from pH7.0 to 9.5, suggesting its practical utility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The influence of EDTA on the detection of Zn2+
ce:section.ce:para: EDTA played a very important role in the detection of Zn2+ using the as-prepared QDs-CDs ratiometric nanosensor. Different concentrations of EDTA made different quenching effect on the nanosensor, and generated different low-level “off” states, eventually affected the sensitivity of the “on-off-on” system. Therefore, various concentrations of EDTA were added into the nanosensors with the same concentration to ascertain the quenching efficiency. Fig. 5 shows that the different EDTA quenched nanosensors had different fluorescence recovery for the same concentration of Zn2+. The inset image shows clearly that the fluorescent intensity at 525 nm decreased as the increase of EDTA level in the range from 1.2 to 1.5 μM. When the concentration of EDTA reached to 1.5 μM, the fluorescence of the QDs was almost quenched, while the concentration of EDTA was low to 1.2 μM, the fluorescence recovery of the nanosensor was not satisfied. In view of that mentioned above, 1.3 μM EDTA was selected as an appropriate concentration in the detection of Zn2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the QDs-CDs ratiometric nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the selectivity of the QDs-CDs ratiometric nanosensor to detect Zn2+, some other metal ions and common substance in biofluid, such as Fe3+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Na+, K+,Pb2+, Al3+, Ag+, NH4 +, HCO3−, urea and BSA, were separately added to the nanosensor quenched by EDTA. As shown in Fig. 6 , when 10 μM Zn2+ is present, the fluorescent intensity of the nanosensor quenched by EDTA increases greatly compared with blank. However, Fe3+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Ag+ and BSA (40 μM), Ca2+, Mg2+, Na+, K+, Pb2+, Al3+, NH4 +, HCO3 − and Urea (100 μM) almost do not interfer the fluorescent intensity of the nanosensor quenched by EDTA, and the fluorescence intensity ratios of the nanosensor were almost unchanged. The result showed that the nanosensor could selectively recognize Zn2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Zn2+ using the QDs-CDs ratiometric nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Detection of Zn2+ using the QDs-CDs ratiometric nanosensor was conducted under the optimal conditions, which the detection pH was 9.0; the EDTA concentration was 1.3 μM and the best response time (Figs. S2, S3). Fig. 7 (A) shows that the fluorescence intensity of the QDs-CDs ratiometric nanosensor at 525 nm continuously increases along with the increase of the concentration of Zn2+ while the fluorescence intensity of the QDs-CDs ratiometric nanosensor at 450 nm remained almost unchanged. The change of the fluorescence intensity ratio of the nanosensor at 525 nm and 450 nm (ΔF525/F450) was proportional to the concentration of Zn2+ ranging from 0.50 μM to 40 μM. The regression equation was ΔF525/F450 = 0.1955[Zn2+] + 0.1409, with a linear correlation coefficient of 0.9978. The limit of detection based on 3σ/k was 0.33 μM, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank (n = 10) and k is the slope of the calibration line. As shown in Table S1, the proposed method has better linear range and LOD compared with the other methods. Fig. 7(B) shows the corresponding color changes of the nanosensor from blue to green with the addition of different concentrations of Zn2+. As shown in Table 1 , the intra-day and inter-day relative standard deviations (RSDs) of three different concentrations of Zn2+ were all less than 10%. These results indicated that the method displays a relatively broad linear range and good precision.

To verify the practical applicability of the proposed method, the QDs-CDs ratiometric nanosensor was applied to the direct determination of Zn2+ in human urine and plasma samples by using standard addition method. The results are summarized in Table 2 . It showed clearly that the recoveries were in the range of 84.89–108.1% with RSDs less than 10%, indicating the practicalutility of the ratiometric nanosensor for detection of Zn2+ in real samples.

In a word, a simple ratiometric “on-off-on” fluorescent nanosensor was developed for the detection of Zn2+ in biological fluids. The fluorescence emission of the nanosensor was turned off with the addition of EDTA and turned on with the addition of Zn2+. Compared with the reported methods for detection of Zn2+ [35,36], the proposed nanosensor has some advantages: (1) the synthesis of the nanosensor is simple, convenient and low cost. (2) the ratiometric nanosensor has good stability to adapt to different pH environments. (3) the “on-off-on” system based on ratiometric nanosensor has good sensitivity and selectivity. The accuracy and precision of the method built for detection of Zn2+ by using the as-prepared ratiometric nanosensor meet the needs of biosamples. The proposed nanosensor was applied to the direct determination of Zn2+ in human urine and plasma samples with satisfactory results, indicating its promising practicability in clinical practice.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 61775099 and No. 21705080).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2018.01.074.
